summary: Patrick McKenzie reports his first sale of Bingo Card Creator, which came unexpectedly on a weekend just over two weeks after launch. The sale was at full price through his website, netting $23.93 after PayPal fees. He notes a Google tracking misconfiguration prevented him from identifying the conversion source. The profit from this single sale covered about 80% of his first month's advertising budget.
key_points: First sale arrived two weeks and one day after launch, on a weekend he nearly turned off ads for
Net profit of $23.93 after PayPal fees on a $24.95 sale, with zero hosting costs via free Payloadz tier
A tracking misconfiguration meant he lost attribution data on his first paying customer
The single sale nearly covered his entire monthly advertising spend, suggesting very low initial ad budget
money_quotes: That was quite unexpected (I had almost turned off my ads for the weekend on the assumption they would not lead to money).
For those keeping track, thats two weeks and a day after launch.
At the moment (with a free Payloadz account until I get, hmm, 3 more sales), that means I clear 24.95 – 1.02 (paypal fees) = $23.93 profit.
Thats about 80% of my advertising budget for my first month, which is now half completed.
key_insight: Even a single sale at a modest price point can nearly recoup early advertising costs when you keep your overhead minimal, validating the microISV model of starting extremely lean.
